steps.text: Be cognizant of compounds that interfere with target analytes.
Analyze a humidified (40 to 50% RH) reference standard in duplicate containing all target compounds at approximately 100 to 500 pptv each.
Verify that the target compounds in the sample collected through the sampling device are within ± 15% of the expected concentrations.
Consult the instrument manufacturer to determine the necessary steps to eliminate the bias.

Test all ports, transfer lines, and connections of the autosampler after calibrating the analytical system and prior to conducting qualifications, or upon replacement of transfer lines, or after analysis of potentially contaminating samples.
Connect humidified (40 to 50% RH) HCF zero air to each port and verify that the concentration for each target compound is < 20 pptv or undetected.
Challenge each port of the autosampler with a reference standard (approximately 100 to 500 pptv) to verify that the autosampler is not causing bias.
Maintain a record of the results for all canisters used for this method.
Qualify each canister as being acceptably leak-tight (new canisters before initial use, and all canisters at least annually).
Evacuate the canister to high vacuum ≤ 0.0067 kPa absolute (0.002 in. Hg or 0.001 psia) or pressurize the canister with clean fill gas to > 203 kPa absolute (60 in. Hg or 29.4 psia).
Close the valve and attach a vacuum/pressure gauge.
Open the valve and record the initial pressure reading.
Treat field spikes identically to field-collected samples in the field and laboratory, including pressure checks and MFCD leak checks.
Analyze the field spikes according to Method 327.
Analyze the field duplicate during the same analytical sequence as the accompanying field sample.
Flag associated results to indicate if the RPD indicates poor method precision.
Analyze at least one replicate analysis for each set of field-collected samples.
Flag associated results to indicate if the RPD indicates poor method precision.
Connect the clean humidified gas sample to the pre-concentrator.
Analyze the humidified zero air to demonstrate cleanliness of the analytical instrument and all connections.
Verify that any detected target compounds in the zero-air challenge sample are at response levels < 20 pptv or not detected.
If target compounds are detected at or above 20 pptv, take steps to remove contamination attributable to the analytical instrumentation by following the manufacturer’s instructions.
Remove canister from service.
Repair canister connections and/or valve.
Perform canister zero-air verification by filling with humidified zero air.
Analyze canister after a minimum of 24 hours and after 7 days to verify each target VOC's concentration is <= 20 pptv at 101.3 kPa absolute (14.7 psia).
Clean and retest canisters that fail the zero-air verification.
Remove canisters from service that cannot pass the zero-air verification after the cleaning process.
Remove the contamination attributable to the sampling unit (e.g., purging with HCF zero air overnight or longer).
Disassemble and clean (see Section 8.6 of this method).
Analyze an instrument blank (IB) injection where no sample or standard is introduced to the preconcentrator.
If IB acceptance criteria are not met, repeat IB analysis.
If IB criteria are still not met, bakeout preconcentrator system and repeat IB analysis.
If IB criteria are still not met, replace contaminated tubing/traps as needed.
Analyze Method blank (MB) canister filled with clean, humidified diluent gas.
If MB acceptance criteria are not met, repeat analysis.
If MB criteria are still not met, reprepare the MB canister and reanalyze.
If MB criteria are still not met, check the system for leaks.
Prepare one CB with each set of calibration standard canisters and analyze with each ICAL.
If CB acceptance criteria are not met, reanalyze CB.
If CB criteria are still not met, reprepare CB and ICAL canister standards.
Calculate the Final Concentration of the Diluted Standard (Cf) using Equation 7: Cf = (Cs * Fs) / (Fs + Fd).

preconditions.text: Operating in MS modes that do not provide full-scan ion
Prior to initial use and as part of an instrument's annual calibration
If you identify problems such as poor response, peak absence, or minimal peak area
If you use an autosampler to facilitate analysis of multiple canisters
After establishing the target pressure in the canister
The RPD of the precision measurements does not agree within ± 30% when both measurements are ≥ 5 times the MDL.
Perform at installation prior to initial use of the instrument.
Initially upon receipt in the laboratory and every 6 months thereafter.
Prior to ICAL and at the beginning of an analytical sequence.
Prior to and following the ICAL and daily following the IB/BFB and prior to the initial daily CCV/SSCV.

constraints.text: Condensed water within the canister can result in corrosion of the interior surface of canisters with weak or deficient coatings and can result in the partitioning of hydrophilic polar VOCs to liquid water.

Moisture in the sample gas may interfere with VOC analysis by GC-MS. Poor or inconsistent water management during preconcentration can cause peak broadening and RT shifts that can result in peak misidentification.
Carbon dioxide in the collected sample can coelute with more volatile VOCs eluting early in the GC-MS run and interfere with their quantitation.

Artifacts in chromatograms, such as silanol compounds formed from the breakdown of silicon-ceramic linings of canisters and siloxane compounds from the breakdown of the stationary phase in an analytical column, can interfere with identification and quantitation of less volatile VOCs.
The volume of air analyzed must be consistent with the laboratory's typical canister sample injection volume.

You must use specially prepared canisters at least 6-liters volume in size that are suitable for trace gas analysis of the target compounds. The canister must include a fixed on/off valve made from chromatographic-grade stainless with metal valve seal surfaces. Each canister must also include a permanent alpha-numeric serial number for identification purposes.
Canisters should be handled with care to ensure that the interior canister surface is not compromised, the valve-to-canister connection remains intact, and weld integrity is maintained.
Excessive torque on unbraced canister valve stems when making connections may cause damage and potentially leaks in the valve stem weld or at the ferrule sealing the canister valve and canister stem.
Shocks resulting in dents to the surface of the canister may damage welds or create small cracks in the interior canister surface that may expose active sites.
You must not use any canister with dents or compromised welds.
Do not use nitrogen for zero-air verification.
Each target VOC’s concentration must be < 20 pptv.
Each target VOC’s concentration must be < 20 pptv and demonstrate acceptably low carryover in the analytical system.
CB must be sufficiently clean such that little or no positive bias is imparted to the calibration.

The equation assumes the density of water to be 1 g/mL and that 100% of the added water to the canister is in the gas phase. The equation does not correct the density of water for the ambient temperature.
